sections.title: Dataset Overview

sections.blurb: The autonomous-system graph school traffic crosses to leave the country. Nodes are networks; edges are observed links.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.title: Network Topology Graphs

sections.blurb: Hop counts from schools to domestic versus foreign servers. Fewer hops mean more direct routes.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html: Path length counts the intermediate hops, at both the IP and autonomous-system level, that school traffic traverses before reaching its destination. Longer paths generally correlate with higher latency and more opportunities for packet loss. The table below shows mean / median across local (domestic M-Lab server) and international destinations.
sections.title: Average Path Length: Local vs International

sections.blurb: How often traffic crosses an Internet Exchange Point, where networks peer locally instead of routing abroad.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html: Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) are physical locations where networks interconnect and exchange traffic directly, often reducing latency and transit costs. A traceroute is counted as crossing an IXP when at least one hop falls within a known IXP prefix. The crossing fraction $f_{\text{IXP}}$ is defined as the ratio of tests with at least one IXP hop to the total number of tests. Higher values indicate that school traffic benefits from local peering infrastructure.
sections.title: IXP Crossing Analysis

sections.blurb: The foreign countries school traffic transits before reaching its destination. More countries mean more external dependence.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html: When a traceroute hop is geolocated or registered to a country other than the origin or destination, that country is a transit country: its infrastructure carries the traffic. Heavy dependence on a single transit country creates geopolitical and reliability risk. Policy changes or outages there can affect all international school connectivity. Two methods are used: IP geolocation of hop addresses, and the registration country of each hop's autonomous system.
sections.title: Transit Country Dependency

sections.blurb: How concentrated international routes are across transit providers. High concentration is a resilience risk.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
This measures how centralized a country's international routing is. Chokepoint coverage is the share of international tests whose path includes the single most-traversed transit AS. The egress Herfindahl-Hirschman Index partitions tests by one representative egress operator each and is defined as

$$\mathrm{HHI} = \sum_{i} s_i^2$$

where $s_i$ is operator $i$'s share of tests. $\mathrm{HHI}$ near $1$ means a single operator carries essentially all traffic. We report three egress views: the first observed transit AS, the first AS located outside the country (physical border), and the first foreign-registered AS (ownership). Domestic hops are often unresolved, so the first observed transit can already be a foreign operator.
sections.title: International Transit Concentration

sections.blurb: Packet loss along measured paths, and how it tracks with path length and distance.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html: Packet loss is measured at each traceroute hop as the fraction of probes that receive no response. This section examines how loss rates vary with path length (number of AS hops) and geographic distance. Longer, more geographically dispersed paths typically experience higher cumulative loss, but loss concentrated at short paths may indicate access-layer or last-mile issues rather than backbone congestion.
sections.title: Loss Rate Analysis

sections.blurb: Round-trip time, throughput, and loss per school. Flags the underserved.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html: This section profiles connectivity quality at the level of individual schools, aggregating RTT, throughput, and loss metrics per school IP. Schools in the top quartile of both RTT and loss are flagged as underserved, a heuristic that surfaces schools with poor path quality on multiple dimensions simultaneously. These schools may benefit most from targeted infrastructure investment or routing improvements.
sections.title: Per-School Performance

sections.blurb: How performance shifts by hour and day of week, in local time.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html: Network performance for schools often varies systematically with time of day and day of week, reflecting shared infrastructure contention during school hours or business peaks. This section shows how test volume, RTT, throughput, and loss shift across hours and days. Pronounced diurnal patterns can indicate congestion during peak hours; weekend vs. weekday differences may reflect shared community or campus network usage.
sections.title: Temporal Patterns

sections.blurb: Route stability and health over the period.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html: Path quality encompasses several complementary metrics. Reachability measures whether traceroutes successfully reach the destination AS; gaps indicate routing black-holes or firewall drops. The geographic detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ compares the total geolocated hop distance $d_{\text{path}}$ to the straight-line great-circle distance $d_{\text{gc}}$ between the school and server; $\rho = 1.0$ is a perfectly direct route, and larger values indicate circuitous routing (tromboning). Path stability captures how often the same school-to-server pair uses different AS-level routes across repeated tests.
sections.title: Path Quality

sections.blurb: Where latency accumulates: domestic access versus international transit.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html: RTT decomposition attributes total round-trip latency to individual transit autonomous systems and countries by computing the incremental delay added at each hop. This pinpoints which network operators or geographic segments contribute the most to observed latency. The detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ (path distance over great-circle distance) contextualises whether latency stems from physical distance or routing inefficiency.
sections.title: RTT Decomposition
